does: EvoRubric introduces a single-policy co-evolutionary reinforcement learning framework that simultaneously generates both responses and evaluation rubrics for open-ended text generation tasks. The method alternates between a Reasoner component that generates responses and a Rubric Generator that creates evaluation criteria, both sharing a unified parameterized policy. To maintain signal reliability, the framework employs a multi-level verification pipeline including a meta-verifier, zero-variance pruning, and Leave-One-Out peer consensus mechanisms. Validated criteria are dynamically archived into a memory pool to provide dense, evolving reward signals without relying on static human annotations or external proprietary models.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in rubric-based reinforcement learning for LLMs, which has moved beyond simple scalar rewards toward explicit criteria-based evaluation for open-ended generation. It addresses a key limitation in existing approaches like those using fixed human-designed rubrics or expensive external models for criterion generation. The paper extends co-evolutionary and self-improving paradigms in machine learning, where multiple components iteratively refine each other. It connects to broader literature on combating reward hacking through verification and consensus mechanisms, techniques previously applied in other domains seeking to prevent policy collapse.
enables: This framework enables more autonomous and adaptive alignment of language models for subjective generation tasks without manual rubric engineering or proprietary external evaluators. The dynamic memory pool of validated criteria could facilitate transfer learning across diverse generation tasks. Future work might leverage the evolved rubrics to improve transparency in model evaluation or to develop curricula that progressively increase task difficulty. The approach also opens directions for studying how co-evolutionary dynamics affect convergence properties and whether the generated rubrics capture human-aligned values or drift toward unexpected heuristics, informing safer RLHF practices more broadly.
